title: We reverse the weave at the OUTER window; the ROM reverses it at the INNER window
claim: P.INER (RBARON.MAC:2794-2797) is reached only when |position| < P.ILIM, and it does `LDA FLAG+1 / EOR I,0FF / STA FLAG+1 ;REVERSE FLAG (HEAD AWAY FROM CENTER)`. That is the reversal: the plane is turned around when it gets CLOSE TO SCREEN CENTRE, and it then accelerates toward the P.IIDL target (0,48,72,120,192 by level). Outside the outer window the direction is inverted only conditionally, and only to bring an off-screen plane back (RBARON.MAC:2776-2781). enemy.ts:152-154 does the opposite: `if (enemy.x >= olim) heading = -1; else if (enemy.x <= -olim) heading = 1; else heading = deltaX >= 0 ? 1 : -1` — reverse at the OUTER wall, never at the inner. P_ILIM is imported at enemy.ts:38 and read exactly once, in spawn().
reasoning: Consequence: our plane bounces wall-to-wall across the full +/-P_OLIM window at a constant cap, a metronome. The arcade plane threads the inner window, gets kicked away from centre, decelerates in the middle band, and only reverses again on the next centre approach — a genuinely irregular weave whose amplitude and period are BOTH level-dependent (via P.IIDL/P.IDLX). Note also P.IIDL[0] = 0: at GMLEVL 0 the "head away from centre" target speed is ZERO, so the level-0 plane essentially parks near centre — the gentlest possible opener, which we do not reproduce. Subsumed by the EN-015 rewrite; listed separately because it is the single behavioural difference a player would name first.

source.verbatim: STA FLAG+1		;REVERSE FLAG (HEAD AWAY FROM CENTER)

title: Enemy planes never move VERTICALLY — the ROM runs the same weave machine on the Y axis
claim: PLNDEL enters the window machine with `LDX I,2 ;CENTER CROSSED` and loads PLSTAT+8+X = PLSTAT+0A — the Y screen position — biased by HORIZN (0x40 = 64): RBARON.MAC:2747-2754. P.WITR then does DEX/DEX to X=0 and `JMP P.WINDW` (RBARON.MAC:2865-2873), re-running the whole zone/target/servo machine on the X axis. UPDPLN:2582-2611 integrates the Y delta (PLSTAT+0E/+0F) into the Y position every frame and clamps it to PFPLOW = 0x80*4 = 0x200 = 512 and PFPHI = 0x140*4 = 0x500 = 1280. Our Enemy.y is set once in spawn() (enemy.ts:128, a +/-40 random) and step() carries it through untouched — our planes fly on a perfectly flat plane forever.
reasoning: Half the arcade's evasion is vertical. A plane that only slides left-right is a fairground duck. Part of the EN-015 rewrite.

title: The plane never flies PAST you — we floor the depth at MIN_DEPTH and it hovers there forever
claim: When a plane's motion depth (PLSTAT+4/+5) crosses below P.MNDP, UPDPLN falls into P.UPD0 and the plane is DESTROYED as an object: `STA PLSTAT+6 ;CLR PLANE` (RBARON.MAC:2741), with PLSTAT+7 = WO.RTN (0x10 = 16 calc frames = 1.54 s) as a re-entry delay, PLVALU zeroed, GMEND0 = 0xC0 arming the returning attack, and MESTM2 = 0x10 setting the BEHIND YOU timer. The plane has flown past your ear and is coming back. enemy.ts:161 instead does `depth: Math.max(enemy.depth - CLOSE_SPEED, MIN_DEPTH)` — the plane parks at depth 140 and weaves in your face indefinitely; nothing in our sim ever retires it or hands it to returning-ace.ts.
reasoning: This is why the returning-ace module is dead code (EN-019): enemy.ts never produces the event that would drive it. The fix is coupled to EN-015 — depth must be an unclamped integrator with a "past you" terminal state, not a clamped decrement.

title: The entire returning-ace mechanic is DEAD CODE — nothing in src/ imports returning-ace.ts
claim: In the ROM the ace pass is wired into the main loop unconditionally: MAIN calls `JSR EOLSEQ ;END OF LIFE SEQUENCE` every calc frame (RBARON.MAC:825), EOLSEQ sprays enemy shells from behind while the returning plane is inbound (RBARON.MAC:1077), and runs the evade check on the exact frame PLSTAT+7 counts down to 0x0C (RBARON.MAC:1078-1079) — i.e. 4 calc frames (384 ms) after the fly-by. In our tree, `grep -rn "closesPast|evadeCheck|beginPass|closeSpeed|P_MNDP" src/` returns hits ONLY inside returning-ace.ts itself. main.ts imports enemy.ts, waves.ts, blimp.ts, guns.ts, flight.ts — never returning-ace.ts. The signature "BEHIND YOU / bank hard to shake him" mechanic — the thing Red Baron is remembered for — is implemented, unit-tested, and never executed.
reasoning: Every constant in returning-ace.ts is therefore moot until it is wired up, which is why I have flagged EN-012/EN-013/EN-014 as fix-worthy anyway: they will be wrong the moment someone connects it. Sizing m because the wiring depends on EN-018 (enemy.ts must first be able to emit a fly-by event).

title: Drones have their own two-phase behaviour (PARALLEL -> FREE); ours weave exactly like leads
claim: A drone is created with status byte = 2 ("FORMATION FLIGHT (=2)", RBARON.MAC:2368/:2382) and X/Y stored as OFFSETS from the lead: PLANE1 = .WORD -100,100 and PLANE2 = .WORD -100,-100 (RBARON.MAC:2480-2481) — hex, so (-256, +256) and (-256, -256). Parallel drones do not steer at all; they are rigidly pinned to the lead. When the lead rotates away, UPDMOB calls FREPAR (RBARON.MAC:2653), and FRDRNE tests `CMP I,2 ;PARALLEL` then `LSR ZX,PLOBDB+6 ;FREE DRONE` (2 >> 1 = 1) and converts the offsets to ABSOLUTE positions (RBARON.MAC:3511-3528). From then on the drone runs UPDRNE, which is a completely different stepper: it creeps at a FIXED +/-0x10 = 16 units per calc frame toward a hard-coded destination — DRPOST = .WORD 160,300,-180,380 (RBARON.MAC:3135), hex, so drone 0 heads for (352, 768) and drone 1 for (-384, 896) — with a screen clamp at 0x05C0 and an optional "growth" phase (Z decremented 0x60 = 96/frame down to 0x0E00) for the DRNSET-selected drone. Our enemy.ts has an EnemyKind discriminant but step() (enemy.ts:148) ignores it entirely, and main.ts:395 maps EVERY enemy — leads and drones alike — through the same weave.
reasoning: No constant in enemy.ts expresses "rigid formation offset from a parent" or "creep toward a fixed destination", so there is nothing to compare against; this is genuinely absent. Three distinct steppers are needed (lead weave / parallel drone / free drone) where we have one. Part of the EN-015 rewrite; sized l on its own because the parallel->free promotion needs a lead-relative coordinate mode that our flat Enemy record cannot hold.

title: The 90-degree entry bank is discarded on the very first step()
claim: STPLNE sets PLSTAT+15 = 0x02 or 0xFE — `SET ROTATION (X/Z) 90 DEGREES` (RBARON.MAC:2307) — and status D5/D4, and gives the plane PLSTAT+7 = 0x20 = 32 calc frames (3.07 s) "TO CROSS CENTER" (RBARON.MAC:2361). While D5 is set, PLNDEL returns immediately (RBARON.MAC:2743-2746) — the plane does NOT weave — and UPDMOB rolls the rotation back toward level at +/-0x40 = 64 units per calc frame (RBARON.MAC:2638/:2641) until it reaches zero, at which point FORMTN clears D5 and the weave begins. The entry is a multi-second banked arc. Our spawn() sets `bank: side * SPAWN_BANK` (pi/2) and `deltaX: 0` (enemy.ts:134-135), but step() unconditionally overwrites bank with `biplaneBank(deltaX)` on the FIRST frame — so our 90-degree entry bank exists for exactly zero frames of motion and is never seen.
reasoning: Also: the arcade plane enters with a real entry velocity, MAXDEL = -0x180 = -384 (RBARON.MAC:470, written to both the X and Y deltas at RBARON.MAC:2298-2309), not the 0 we spawn with. Two separate misses — no rollout state machine, and no entry velocity.

title: The blimp can appear from the very first wave — the ROM locks it behind four planes
claim: The 25 % roll is guarded: `LDA N.PLNZ / CMP I,4 / BCC 25$` (RBARON.MAC:2325-2327) — N.PLNZ counts every plane that has appeared this game (incremented at RBARON.MAC:2398), and the blimp cannot spawn until FOUR planes have been fielded. Our shouldSpawnBlimp(roll) (blimp.ts:94-95) is the bare 25 % roll with no counter gate at all, so a blimp can be the first thing a player ever sees. The ROM also picks the entry side deterministically from the player's own turn (`BIT PLDELX / JSR DPABS ;-X SIDE`, RBARON.MAC:1429-1430) at |X| = 0x03C0 = 960; our spawn() (blimp.ts:107-108) picks the side from the Rng at |X| in [180, 300).
reasoning: Two separate divergences on the same call: a missing unlock gate and a wrong entry geometry. Both are small; the unlock gate needs a plane counter threaded from main.ts.

title: The blimp fires 2x too fast and has no level gate — ROM is 1-in-4 frames AND requires GMLEVL >= 2
claim: The blimp does not use PLNSHL. BLMOTN calls `JSR SHLAUN ;LAUNCH SHELL @ PLAYER` (RBARON.MAC:4229), and SHLAUN opens `LDA FRAME / AND I,3 ;1 OUT OF 4 FRAMES / BEQ SHLAU0` (RBARON.MAC:4027-4030) — one shot per FOUR calc frames = 10.4167 / 4 = 2.604 shots/s. SHLAU0 then hard-gates on level: `LDX GMLEVL / DEX / DEX / BMI SHLAUX ;NO GROUND SHELLS @ LOWER LEVELS` (RBARON.MAC:4038-4041) — GMLEVL < 2 fires NOTHING. Our blimpFires(frame) (blimp.ts:143-145) is `(frame & 1) === 0` — one shot per TWO frames = 5.208 shots/s, exactly 2x the arcade rate — with no level gate whatsoever.
reasoning: The module header at blimp.ts:20-23 explicitly claims the opposite as a design fact: "unlike a plane, whose @ PLAYER bit is PLNLVL level-gated, the blimp is a threat whenever it is present... a menace even in the early sky the planes leave quiet." The source says the reverse: at GMLEVL 0-1 the blimp is completely harmless, and it cannot even spawn before four planes have flown. Our blimp is roughly the most dangerous thing in the early game; in the arcade it is the least. Doubly wrong, and it lands precisely in the early-game window a new player judges the game by.

ours.verbatim: return (Math.floor(frame) & 1) === 0 // ÷2 FRAME cadence — fire on even frames

title: The blimp is an APPROACHING airship on a 2.9-second fuse, not a constant-depth lateral drifter
claim: INITBP puts the blimp at Z = 0x1000 = 4096 (BLOBJ+5 = 0x10, +4 = 0; RBARON.MAC:1425-1426) and gives it an X velocity of 0xFFF8 = -8 units/frame, sign-flipped by the entry side (RBARON.MAC:1443-1448). BLMOTN then decrements Z by a 16-bit -0x0080 = -128 EVERY calc frame (`ADC I,-80` / `ADC I,-1`, RBARON.MAC:4260-4265) and clears the object when the Z MSB drops below 1, i.e. Z < 0x100 = 256 (RBARON.MAC:4266-4270). So the blimp's whole life is (4096 - 256) / 128 = 30 calc frames = 30 x 96 ms = 2.88 seconds, during which it BEARS DOWN on you at 128/frame while sliding sideways at only 8/frame — a 16:1 closing-to-drift ratio. Ours (blimp.ts:54, :57, :130) cruises at a CONSTANT depth of 600, drifts at 12/frame, never closes, and never expires — a 0:12 closing-to-drift ratio, i.e. the exact inverse of the arcade motion.
reasoning: blimp.ts:12-17 states as design intent that "the blimp does the opposite [of the plane]: ONE steady drift... and cruises at a constant depth; the motion is purely lateral." That is a fabrication — the ROM blimp is almost purely a closer. The module header is candid that BLMOTN was "not byte-transcribed in the quarry" and that the depth/drift were invented; it is byte-transcribable, and the invention is backwards. Player-visible effect: our blimp floats past as scenery, the arcade blimp is a timed threat you must destroy before it reaches you.

title: The blimp YAWS as it approaches; our Blimp has no heading at all
claim: INITBP sets a Y-axis (heading) angle of +/-0x0200 at BLOBJ+14/+15 (`STA BLOBJ+15 ;Y ANGLE MSB`, RBARON.MAC:1439-1442), and BLMOTN rotates it toward zero by +/-0x28 = 40 units per calc frame (RBARON.MAC:4281-4297), while YAXIS (RBARON.MAC:4211-4214) applies it to the geometry — the airship swings to face you as it closes. Our Blimp record has `bank` (roll, pinned to 0) and no heading field of any kind, so there is nothing to compare; the rotation simply does not exist in our model.
reasoning: Our `bank: 0` is not itself wrong — the ROM never rolls the blimp either. What is missing is the ORTHOGONAL axis: a yaw that starts at 0x200 and unwinds at 40/frame over the blimp's ~30-frame life (0x200 / 40 = 12.8 frames to level out). Classed NO_COUNTERPART rather than DIVERGENCE because there is no field of ours to cite. Cosmetic relative to EN-024/EN-025; accept unless the blimp is being reworked anyway, in which case it is trivial to add alongside.
